raw_transcript:
Doctor: Good morning, David. You're looking a bit pale today. My nurse mentioned you're having a significant increase in bowel frequency?
Patient: Doctor, it's terrible. I'm going twelve, maybe fifteen times a day. It's almost all blood and urgency. I can't even leave the house because I'm afraid I won't make it to a bathroom in time.
Doctor: Fifteen times a day with gross hematochezia is a medical emergency. Are you having any fevers or a racing heart?
Patient: I feel like my heart is beating out of my chest just walking from the car. I've had some low-grade fevers at night, maybe 100.2.
Doctor: That's a sign of systemic inflammation. You've been on Mesalamine, and we tried a Prednisone taper last month. How did you do on the steroids?
Patient: When I was on 40 milligrams, I felt great. But as soon as I hit 20, everything came back worse than before. I'm currently at 20 and I'm drowning here.
Doctor: This is what we call 'steroid-dependent' or 'steroid-refractory' colitis. Given the frequency and the systemic symptoms, I'm actually going to recommend we admit you to the hospital today for IV steroids.
Patient: The hospital? Is it that bad?
Patient: Maybe a little ringing if I've been at a loud concert, but it goes away.
Doctor: Have you noticed any numbness or tingling in your hands or feet?
Doctor: Are you having any pain or stiffness in your neck or shoulders?
Doctor: Have you noticed any changes in your skin, like unusual dryness or new marks?
Patient: A bit of tension from my desk job, but nothing major.
Patient: I take a multivitamin and some Vitamin D in the winter.
Doctor: Are you currently seeing any other specialists besides me?
Patient: I've gained about ten pounds, but I'm trying to lose it.
Doctor: Any history of seizures, fainting, or blackouts?
Patient: Occasionally in my left foot if I sit in one position for too long.
Patient: No, nothing like that. My heart feels fine.
Doctor: Do you find yourself eating late at night or skipping meals often?
Patient: My mother has a slow thyroid, but as far as I know, I'm okay.
Patient: Just my primary care doctor for my annual checkup.
Patient: Only Penicillin. It gave me a nasty rash when I was a kid.
Doctor: Any history of blood clots or deep vein thrombosis in your family?
Patient: No surgeries other than my appendix when I was twelve.
Patient: I usually have a late snack, but I try to eat three meals.
Patient: No, I haven't left the state in over a year.
Patient: Probably not enough. Maybe three or four glasses. I drink a lot of coffee though.
Doctor: Have you ever had any surgeries on your lungs or heart?
Doctor: Have you noticed any swelling in your legs or ankles at the end of the day?
Patient: Only when I have too much caffeine in the morning.
Doctor: Any history of kidney stones or frequent urinary tract infections?
Patient: I'm a software engineer, so I sit at a desk most of the day.
Patient: Maybe a little, but I usually just think I'm out of shape.
Doctor: Any history of depression or anxiety that required medication?
Doctor: How about your blood pressure? Do you monitor that at home?
Patient: It's hit or miss. Some days I'm starving, other days the smell of food makes me nauseous.
Doctor: Any history of glaucoma or cataracts?
Doctor: Yes. We need to stabilize you with IV hydration and high-dose Solu-Medrol. We also need to rule out secondary infections like C. diff or CMV which can complicate a flare.
Patient: What happens if the IV steroids don't work?
Doctor: If we don't see a significant drop in stool frequency within 72 hours, we'll perform a 'rescue' therapy. That usually means either Infliximab or Cyclosporine. We want to avoid a colectomy, which is the surgical removal of the colon, at all costs.
Patient: I really don't want surgery.
Doctor: Neither do we. That's why we're acting aggressively now. I'll have the nurse start an IV here for some fluids, and we'll coordinate your admission across the street. Are you okay with that?
Patient: If it stops the bleeding, yes. Thank you.
Doctor: Of course. We'll get you through this.
corrected_note: Hand-crafted clinical encounter for Ulcerative Colitis - Acute Severe Flare
